forecasts.reasoning: The land border crossings between Benin and Nigeria's Kwara state were closed by Benin on May 7, 2025, in response to the active movement of a newly emerged terrorist group, "Mahmuda," which is attempting to cross into Benin due to Nigerian military pressure. Since the initial closure, Benin has escalated its clampdown by extending border closures to Lagos and waterway routes to Ogun state as late as May 21, 2025. Given that the knowledge cutoff is May 31, 2025, there is only one month left for the borders to reopen to satisfy the resolution criteria. Border closures in West Africa prompted by active militant groups or arms smuggling historically last for several months or years (e.g., Nigeria's 2019-2020 closures, Benin-Niger closures since 2023). While Nigerian legislators passed a motion on May 13 urging a diplomatic resolution, there is no evidence that the militant threat has been neutralized or that diplomatic talks have progressed sufficiently to reverse an expanding blockade. Finding a resolution in such a short window (before July 1, 2025) is therefore highly unlikely.

Between April and May 2025, France actively spearheaded an initiative to officially recognize a Palestinian state alongside the UK and Canada. This diplomatic push is explicitly targeted to culminate at a UN conference co-hosted by France and Saudi Arabia scheduled for June 2025 (likely June 17-20). While some mid-May reports featured UK officials suspecting Macron might delay the announcement, subsequent statements on May 20 confirmed a strong trilateral commitment to prepare the recognition. A May 28 update indicated French officials are still 'weighing up the move' ahead of the conference, injecting some uncertainty regarding whether formal execution will happen in June or be delayed. However, the multi-national coordination, high-level public commitments (including from PM Bayrou and FM Lammy), and the specific targeting of a June UN conference event provide strong evidence that at least one of these countries will formalize their recognition in June 2025. Given the typical risks of delay in high-stakes Middle East diplomacy, the probability is estimated at a moderately high 71%.
